So that they don't go looking at other metrics that don't matter. If I need to bring in other metrics to support what I'm saying, I will. But we agree on so we can't keep losing goal posts of what's important. We send out weekly reports to them to kind of give them an idea of where we're at. If we're not where we need to be, we provide some insights. If we are where we want to be, then we're good to go. And we just share the good news with them. But we usually send out weekly reports. We don't automate them because we want clients to know that we're looking at them. And provide some context for what we're going to do weekly. Even if it's just a couple sentences, or even if it's just like, hey.
After working, we're going to leave it alone for a week and then come back next week. um, have access to us, access to me via Slack. i have a project manager. i have marketing coordinators and stuff like that. i have a creative director. i have other people on the team that can handle. the bulk of the communications. if they need me, i have a meeting link, they can book a time. You know, and if you don't set that, they're going to think that, you know, everything under, they, they need everything under the sun. So you know, a lot of times they'll get technical questions on calls and they don't always have a good answer if you're. So I'm just curious about that. You are not unique in your challenge.
I was literally on the phone with, I'm on the North Atlantic Council for Partners with HubSpot. And there were like dozens of agency owners on that last week. Every single person on that call, whether it was an agency of five or agency of 100, had the same challenge. It's really about. Having enough reps, I think. A lot of people take certifications and do things and don't know why they're doing them. And some people just never learn the right, never learn strategy. Um... I think even some strategists don't know strategy very well. I. I work with the only strategist I hire personally— people I know. Um. And I know that's not scalable at all. But, um... You just have to interview very well and it's hard.
Give them a bunch of test projects and pay them for them. And just see what their approach is. Don't touch an ad account. I don't need you pushing the buttons. If I had a, let's say, you get like a plumber. Give them a test. If I were a plumber and I had an ad budget of X, Y, and Z, and just do a sample website, like go out there and find someone's website and say, 'All right.' This plumber's out here. What would your approach be? Right. And then have them build out. A plan with a certain budget based on their goals. And make them ask questions. Like for me, it's not so much having the right answers. Do they know how to get asked the right questions? Two.
In order to come up with the right strategy. As well. And then treat yourself when you interview them, treat them like a client. Because really they need to be able to, the client needs to have confidence that they know what they're doing or else they're never going to talk to them. They're going to want to talk to you all the time, right? Um, I was having that issue. I'd have strategists on my team, and then it was like, The client would just text me or call me. And I'm like, 'Well, so-and-so doesn't know what they're talking about.' And I don't want to waste time and talk to them. I want to talk to you because you know what's going on. I'm like. Great. You know, but now I have a creative director.
I have a solutions architect. I have a lead developer. I have people who are smarter than me in these areas that can really lead. Not only the clients, but our organization, and you know, uh, doing better work and so on and so forth. So it is not easy to hire strategists. Because there's a lot of people who think they're strategists that aren't. But you first and foremost you got to recognize what a strategist is. It isn't— it takes some trial and error. I'm sure I just isn't someone who's scaled someone's company from 100 bucks to a million. That's not a strategist. That's someone who had a great campaign. A strategist knows. Why the campaign did well, how to replicate those results across different clients and different et cetera, et cetera.
They know how to fix it when things go wrong. Um... Yeah, they know a lot of those things. And so, a lot of people who've had accidental success online think they're strategists and they're not. Yeah. Yeah, here you are. Yeah, so if you know, if you know the difference, once you know the difference between a strategist and an implementer, um... Like, someone should be able to build a report from AdWords data, right? That's not necessarily strategist, but I call that like a QA type person, analyst type person. They need to be able to build out reports, like ad hoc reports and stuff like that. For clients, let's say, you know, ask some problem-solving questions like, okay, so um We launched a campaign and... Google is showing zero conversions.
What could the problem be? You know, there's one of 30 things it could be. You know, and if they're saying the first thing they say is, 'Oh, well, the campaign probably sucks,' we need to do a new campaign. They're probably not a strategist. You got it. They don't understand tracking. They don't understand. You know, there's probably something broken. You know, if they're listing those types of things, they're like troubleshooting methodology, then they're probably closer to strategists than— at least from the technical side, the implementation side. From like an actual like marketing side. Like. That gets tricky because a lot of ads people don't actually understand business. or marketing. Most of them don't. And it's not just understanding how Facebook ad platform platforms work, but it's like.
understanding consumer behavior, understanding what drives conversion, what understand what differentiation looks like, how to do market research. how to take that market research and make decisions and come up with ideas and all those things. It's not easy. Yeah. Okay. So it sounds like you get a lot of, so you've, so. Asking the right questions in the interview process sounds like a big one. And it sounds like you also get a lot of people out of networking, or you hire people through just good old-fashioned networking. Okay. I'm very picky about who I hire as a strategist because... They're also usually leading teams and telling people what to do. Like. So they have to be good communicators. They have to be, they have to have some leadership training.
So not just like, oh, I've led a bunch of people. They have to have gone through some sort of actual. like leadership training, gone to a leadership retreat of some sort, been taught by somebody I recognize as a leadership training expert. In order for me to bring them on as a strategist. Just because a lot of times you bring on higher-level people and they don't have the team building skills and the people skills to work with clients or employees, and then it creates all kinds of problems. So. It's a big role. Strategy isn't just— lone wolf stuff. Strategy it's very much, very hands-on. That's why you get paid really well to do strategy. Um, but again, most people don't have that capability. Appreciate that. Thank you. That's definitely tough.
For those of you that have your own business and you're not running an agency. Um, one of the questions that we get a lot during Scalable is, 'Like, hey, I'm a marketing director, but they don't know how to run reports or they're just not cutting it or they're just like.' That is very, very common. A lot of times you have a very strong implementer-type person. That's kind of what Matthew's talking about. He had a question about his team and his team dynamic and his team structure. And um, we were talking about that there's people who are training, those are like interns, and you have implementation people who strictly are fantastic at implementing, they follow SOPs. But then you have people who understand how to analyze things. And stuff like that.
Um, and then you have strategists and managers. You got to identify. A lot of times, people who are in the trainee or elementary phase are brought into these higher-level roles without the right training, without the right guidance, without the right anything. They're expected to put ideas in place and put marketing strategies in place, and that's not necessarily their strong suit. So, if you know of anyone that's having that issue, that's usually what the culprit is. And then the managers and strategy people are very hard to hire. Um. Hire slow. Fire fast, they always say. If it takes you a month to get a strategist in, take that month. Especially if you're a small agency, it's not super funded. Because you're going to spend a lot of money on that talent. Um. Just make sure that they're, uh, they have the right training and they have the right aptitude to be able to do that job well. Great questions, Matthew, for sure.

And so a lot of those frameworks where we're having to hire Teams that. aren't able to do stuff that's not already out there. I'm trying to say it nicely. Um. It's really hard. They don't do very well.
working with us so we gave up trying to work with those companies that have those teams where Um, like you have your web team, right? Like if I don't want to hire a web team internally, I can go hire this person's fractional web team where they just— I pay them a flat fee and they just do all of our web work. None of those worked out. Same with our graphic design stuff. None of it worked out. Same with our ad stuff. None of that worked out. So I completely gave up on those just because they just. They weren't high-level enough. They wanted to do stuff for like hair salons and stuff. And I was like, I can't. It works for doctors and lawyers.
Plumbers and all those things— it doesn't work when you're trying to do stuff in like neuro neurology and accounting. In you know, disruptive industries where it's really specialized and you need like technical technical copywriters and experts and stuff. And that's what we work with. So. Rajka, it's Lee here. I'm familiar with that, what you're speaking about with the Ryan Levesque. I think it was a webinar maybe. And I think it was actually really interesting to look at. And I think even if you don't go through and actually create fractional teams, it would be worth listening to. I don't have the link for it. I don't know if you have something to share, maybe to Matthew or something. But what I found interesting about it was it just gave, it expanded your perspective on how you could do team.
And especially when you're in a point of growth. You know, it's not the traditional way of building a team and building an organization. And it just provided a really good perspective on what you could do from a fractional perspective, which is what most of us, you know, haven't done or, you know, we don't have that in our background. So it would even be worth, I think, watching or listening to just. to have that different perspective and see how that could possibly work. For yourself. The link for you so what I do is I send them this form. This isn't this form— I can't find my new one. It's all automated now. But basically, they fill this out. This actually creates an automation and it goes to my HubSpot account. And then it creates a to-do list for me. On my project management. But this is like, if we do hire them, I have all their info. I don't have to chase them down for it later. Um and then it kind of talks about like what kind of work suits them so even if they're doing like advertising or development or something There's a couple of different areas to kind of see.
Where they like to live, work preference, part-time, full-time, remote, and office. And they can choose multiple. Um. I have them do a personality test, which is interesting. It's actually kind of fun. It's a 16 personality test. It's kind of cool to see like how our team is built up. Most of the people on our team are introverts, hardcore introverts. There's like one or two besides me— extroverts. But it's kind of cool to see kind of like who you attract. Um, or whatever. I have a lot of the rare personalities on my team. Um, which is kind of weird, like one in a hundred. I have like four of those. I'm like, I don't know how I got that, but. It is kind of cool.
Um, And then it's interesting because we have our core values as an agency. Um, but. I have our, like, I asked them to put their core values there. And it's interesting. The people who work out tend to be the ones whose core values match ours. Just off the bat. Yes, very helpful. I need to put something together like this. Yes. And so, um, People who are very, you can tell who the very self-serving people are. I tend to go for entrepreneurial people. Instead of people who just want to show up for a paycheck. Um, and the people who just don't have a paycheck are totally fine. There's nothing wrong with them. They tend to just get their work done and go home. And that's the kind of people I want.
I don't want people working. 20 hours a day, eight days a week. I want people get in and get out, right? Be efficient. Get it done, move on with life, right? It's actually what we do want, but. Um, we have our list of core values, and if at least one matches, that's usually a good sign. Um, What are the three things you wish to learn in your next role? They are super important. Because we want people to grow, and we want to be able to support people on that growth. Um, and so, even if they're an independent contractor, we want to make sure that we provide some value in training. So we have the digital marketer HQ, or not HQ, but the labs and stuff. And so our whole team gets added to that.
Um, for training purposes and stuff. So I also have additional programs and stuff that I've purchased that I give them access to. When they get started, just to kind of help them. Along their next learning path. Also, if you're hiring a strategist and they're trying to learn things that are kind of— I usually, I don't want to hold it against them, but I'll often like. Like if they're trying to learn strategy and they're like, 'Oh, I want to learn how to get, you know, just things that they probably should already know. Then you might see that there's a gap or that they might not actually be a strategist. If they're learning for things like leadership and stuff like that. You can kind of see where there might be some gaps.
That doesn't necessarily mean that they don't know anything, but it's usually a good idea to see if there's any weird red flags there. Um. What makes you feel like you're a good fit for our team? Um. They'll usually do their research and stuff like that and they'll find something. What are you looking for in your next working opportunity? That's also great because. Sometimes there's people that just. Like we're just too small for. And I don't want to waste their time either. So I'll say, you know what? I don't think we're big enough for you. And if they still want to interview, I'll interview them, but. Um. Yeah. And then short-term goals and long-term goals. Um. Okay. Like what's there next? What's the next thing that they want to do?
And then I haven't submitted a video, and a lot of them do. A lot of people that really want the opportunity will submit a short 60-second video. Tell us about who they are. Because I don't feel like resumes really give a full story. Um. Another thing that we do is when we're putting the things out on Indeed, the first test is if they can follow instructions. I think so much has changed, you know, in the last two years— with um, you know, the world shifting obviously to remote. But that whole, you know, what we knew about teams before and resources within that kind of remote distributed world. And then now, where we're at, that a lot of, there's just a lot of shifting going on, I think, within.
How we're building our teams, but also what I'm finding is there's so many more opportunities for candidates out there that I really like. I agree with what Amara was saying— with really putting a tight container around. You know, when you put your job posting up and asking it, like using that as your first test, basically some of those questions or things that you're putting in the job description and really be specific on it. And if they're not answering, you know, you're not, you're not even going to follow up with it. So because I think, now that so many people have the availability or option to work remotely, I'm personally seeing that. the influx of resumes, you know, they're just not a match. But there's so many coming in.
So I find, right from the start, if I can keep to those parameters for myself as well, then it really does shorten the list. saves me a lot of time too, looking at, looking at maybe five candidates versus, you know, the 80 that maybe sent in a resume or something. So. Right. Yeah. You know, it's funny because. There's one thing that people aren't talking about right now. It's that a lot of tech companies just light off at least 7% of their workforce. A lot of startup tech companies like ClickUp just laid off like seven or eight, their bottom 9%. Um. What was the name? There's like. eight other big companies. And this is like, this isn't, this is like 900 people. 900 to 2000 people they've laid off.
So you have a lot of people, all these companies hogged up all these resources. And then the minute they're. like earnings came out or whatever. Their priorities shifted, they just let them all go. Completely. So there was a huge hiring crunch and companies had to figure out how to like. Just get it like. Operate without all the talent. And now all these people. You know, the great resignation, they went on to these bigger and better companies with more salaries, and now they've just all been let go. So. There's a lot of opportunity to hire. I think it's starting to normalize itself out right now because it was. It was the employees' market and now I think it's coming back to we can be more picky again.
Yeah, and I think a lot of it comes to really being specific in your job description, making sure people are following. I think people got used to being able to just, here's my resume, hire me. You know, rather than, you know, like you say, send in a cover letter, answer the questions, maybe provide. It's interesting. You can see the ones that are hungry for it, right? They'll provide an example or, you know, do something really maybe specific to you with their portfolio. So I think if you can kind of get through the weeds with that, there are some really good, there's good talent out there that will match your requirements, but there's almost a strategy involved with them. Reviewing those resumes when they come in.
So I think we're going to see a lot of interesting shifting there this year. I agree with you, Amara. I have, I mean. Hey, and Roshka, if you have anything on that Ryan Levesque fractional training, I'd love to take a look at that too, if you're still there. If we lost him. Thank you. Okay, you can download this. You can always message Rajka, excuse me, in circle as well, Matt. Okay, I'll do that. All right, here's. Here's a link to the questions that we have.
Just a quick thing of what I'm looking for in these questions.
What are you really good at professionally? Um, that's just like... You want them to talk about things that would, you want to listen for things that align with what you need from them on a day-to-day basis. So one of the exercises I do for myself when I'm building out a job description. Because I never just copy and paste the same one each time. I try to make it a little better. I try to think about what am I actually like, what are the tasks I'm going to assign them on day one? What are the things I need for them to be able to do? What is their day-to-day look like? What do they need to be able to do daily? What do they need to be able to do monthly?
What do they need to be able to do weekly, quarterly, annually? I like to list out all those things so that I'm very clear about what I want this person to do because it's so easy as a marketer or if you're hiring a marketing role, there to be a ton of overlap. So it's like, I need someone who can run ads on Facebook, on Instagram, on Google, on TikTok, on Snapchat. I need someone who can set up tracking that knows and understands custom events. I need someone who understands, like, you want to get down to the nitty gritty so that when you're asking people questions, like sometimes I'm the only one to interview people. And sometimes I'll have my creative director on as second or whatever.
And I keep my interviews short, like 30 minutes, 30, 45 minutes. Right. And so a lot of times I'll say, 'Hey, I'm going to do the behavioral interview first and then I'll do the technical interview where I'll ask the very technical questions, make sure that they have the aptitude to be able to do the things that I'm going to ask of them.' So really getting aligned with their strengths. You know, there's also the soft skills that you talked about, but there's also their technical strengths too. I mean, then they're weaknesses. I find that people have to be self-aware. Like if you can't tell me. any kind of, I know all my weaknesses and I wear them like I know exactly what they are. I overcommit.
I could go on and on and on about my weaknesses. I also know, like, I also put guardrails and barriers for myself so that I can. you know, make sure that my weaknesses don't get the best of me and get the best of my team, right? Most people are really bad interviewers too. And so a lot of people have a really hard time with that question. And so I really try to. um push that question in a different way like Hey, we all have weaknesses. I want to just know, like... A, are you aware of them? And B, what do you do to get around them? Or how do you, how do you? I like make sure that they don't interfere with your productivity or your, you know, whatever else you got going on.
Type situation. And some people really just can't answer that question. And it's not that I don't hire them for that. I don't hire them. I won't not hire someone for that. Um, but It is a question that a lot of people just have a really hard time with. Who are your last three bosses and how would they rate your performance? That's a good one. Some people are just eager to answer that question. Some people will just avoid it like the plague. Um, Most challenging team dynamics. If you're hiring a strategy type person, they need to be able to answer that question very well. And they need to have tons of examples. Keep digging on that one. Get three to five examples if you're hiring a strategist. Um.
And then what are the three attributes of a leader that you work best with? I remember one girl— I was hiring a girl for copywriting. And she was just like, 'I don't like working on multiple projects at the same time. I need to work on one thing at a time. Like we're an agency. So I just, I wouldn't, I'm like, we're an agency. I think you would literally drown. And she was really mad that I wouldn't hire. And I'm like. This isn't the environment. You're not going to be able to do your best work in this environment. You're going to get thrown 10,000 projects at a time. We'll give you enough time to do it, but you're not going to just be able to focus on one.
You need to work for a company if you just want to work on one project at a time. And even then, God bless you. Good luck with that. Amara, is it? Sorry to interrupt you. Yeah, you're okay.
